system_prompt:
You are a senior dermoscopy expert and knowledge engineer.

You will be shown a candidate rule that fires correctly on some images (TRUE POSITIVES)
but incorrectly on others (FALSE POSITIVES). Your task is to identify the single most
discriminating visual feature that distinguishes the TP images from the FP images —
i.e., a feature that is consistently present in TPs but absent in FPs, or vice versa.

This feature will be used to tighten the rule's pre-conditions.

Output ONLY a JSON object:
{
  "discriminating_feature": "snake_case_feature_name",
  "description": "Plain-language description of the feature.",
  "present_in": "tp" | "fp",
  "confidence": "high" | "medium" | "low",
  "rationale": "Why this feature distinguishes TPs from FPs."
}

If you cannot identify a reliable discriminating feature, output:
{
  "discriminating_feature": null,
  "description": "Cannot identify a reliable discriminating feature.",
  "present_in": null,
  "confidence": "low",
  "rationale": "Explanation of why the distinction cannot be reliably made."
}
text_content:
Pair: Melanoma vs Melanocytic Nevus
Rule favors: Melanoma

Rule: When a pigmented lesion contains gray-blue or blue-gray structureless areas (not organized into a network, not red-purple lacunae) alongside brown pigmented components and shows asymmetric, multi-zone architecture, classify as Melanoma.

Pre-conditions:
  1. Gray-blue or blue-gray structureless areas are present within the lesion — distinct patches of slate-gray or bluish-gray color without an organized network structure
  2. Brown pigmented components are also present alongside the gray-blue areas (distinguishes from blue nevus, which is entirely blue-gray)
  3. The gray-blue areas occupy only a portion of the lesion — NOT the entire lesion uniformly (uniform steel-blue homogeneous lesion = blue nevus, not melanoma)
  4. Lesion is asymmetric in shape and/or pigment distribution
  5. At least three distinct colors are visible (e.g., light tan/golden brown, dark brown, gray-blue)
  6. No red/purple lacunae (rules out hemangioma as source of blue-gray color)
  7. No seborrheic keratosis features (no milia-like cysts, no comedo-like openings)
  8. Lesion is flat or only minimally elevated — not a raised blue-gray nodule (a raised bluish nodule suggests blue nevus or pigmented nodular basal cell carcinoma rather than melanoma)
  9. No features of dermatofibroma: no shiny central white patch surrounded exclusively by a delicate pigment network restricted to the periphery

TRUE POSITIVE cases (rule fired correctly):
  [TP 1] Ground truth: Melanoma
    Validator observations: The lesion shows: (1) blue-gray/dark blue-purple structureless areas occupying the central portion without organized network; (2) brown pigmented components visible at the periphery and intermediate zones; (3) the blue-gray areas are not uniform across the entire lesion; (4) asymmetric shape and pigment distribution; (5) multiple colors visible including light tan at periphery, medium brown, dark brown, and blue-gray/blue-black centrally; (6) no red/purple lacunae suggesting hemangioma; (7) no milia-like cysts or comedo-like openings suggesting seborrheic keratosis; (8) lesion appears relatively flat; (9) no dermatofibroma pattern. All pre-conditions appear to be met.
  [TP 2] Ground truth: Melanoma
    Validator observations: The lesion shows: (1) gray-blue/blue-gray structureless areas visible in the central-left region; (2) brown pigmented components surrounding and adjacent to the gray-blue areas; (3) the gray-blue areas occupy only a portion of the lesion, not the entire lesion; (4) clear asymmetry in shape and pigment distribution; (5) at least three distinct colors are visible - light pink/tan centrally, dark brown peripherally, and gray-blue in between; (6) no red/purple lacunae suggesting hemangioma; (7) no milia-like cysts or comedo-like openings suggesting seborrheic keratosis; (8) the lesion appears relatively flat; (9) no central white shiny patch with peripheral network suggesting dermatofibroma. All pre-conditions appear to be met.

FALSE POSITIVE cases (rule fired on wrong class):
  [FP 1] Ground truth: Melanocytic Nevus
    Validator observations: The lesion shows: (1) gray-blue/blue-gray structureless areas visible in the central-dark zone without organized network; (2) brown pigmented components surrounding and intermixed with the gray-blue areas; (3) gray-blue areas occupy only a portion of the lesion, not uniformly; (4) the lesion is clearly asymmetric in shape and pigment distribution; (5) at least three distinct colors are visible — light tan/golden-brown peripherally, dark brown, and gray-blue/dark bluish centrally; (6) no red/purple lacunae suggestive of hemangioma; (7) no milia-like cysts or comedo-like openings of seborrheic keratosis; (8) the lesion appears flat/minimally elevated; (9) no dermatofibroma features (no shiny central white patch with peripheral-only delicate network). All pre-conditions are met.

What single visual feature most reliably distinguishes the TP cases from the FP cases? Focus on what the validator *observed* in each case.
